generated_oml:
// C3: Physical Sensing Components
instance OperationalLogSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Collects operational logs and contextual data traces from physical systems for digital shadow aggregation, supporting real-time monitoring and analysis purposes."
    DTDFVocab:producedData OperationalLogDataTrace
]

instance EnvironmentalParameterSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Captures environmental parameters (temperature, humidity, pressure) from physical systems to support contextual data aggregation for digital twin insights."
    DTDFVocab:producedData EnvironmentalParameterDataTrace
]

instance SensorMeasurementAggregator : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Aggregates heterogeneous sensor measurements (implicitly referenced via data traces) from physical objects for digital shadow representation purposes."
    DTDFVocab:producedData AggregatedSensorDataTrace
]

// C4: Physical-to-Virtual Interaction
instance OperationalLogDataTrace : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom OperationalLogSensor
]

instance EnvironmentalParameterDataTrace : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom EnvironmentalParameterSensor
]

instance AggregatedSensorDataTrace : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom SensorMeasurementAggregator
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance DigitalShadowReferenceModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Formalized reference model defining one-way data flows from physical objects to digital representations, establishing the foundation for contextual data aggregation and abstraction."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataAggregationPipeline
]

instance SysMLStateMachineModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Behavioral model using SysML state machines to represent dynamic system states and transitions for digital twin state tracking and anomaly detection."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo BehavioralSimulationEngine
]

instance MontiArcAutomatonModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Component-based behavioral model using MontiArc Automaton for dynamic system behavior representation, supporting compositional modeling and automated verification."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo BehavioralSimulationEngine
]

instance SimulinkHybridModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Physics-based simulation model using MathWorks Simulink for hybrid system dynamics analysis, supporting multi-domain co-simulation with physical twins."
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulationCoSimulationFramework
]

instance OperationalDataTrace : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated and abstracted operational data traces from physical systems, processed through ETL pipelines for digital twin insights generation."
    DTDFVocab:fromData AggregatedSensorDataTrace
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataProcessingEngine
]

instance ContextualDataAggregation : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Structured contextual data derived from physical system traces, enabling purpose-specific analysis and decision support through abstraction mechanisms."
    DTDFVocab:fromData OperationalLogDataTrace, EnvironmentalParameterDataTrace
    DTDFVocab:inputTo InsightGenerationEngine
]

// C11: Enablers
instance DataAggregationPipeline : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "ETL pipeline implementing automated data aggregation and abstraction from raw sensor traces to structured digital twin data representations."
    DTDFVocab:enables MonitoringService, PredictiveAnalyticsService
]

instance DataProcessingEngine : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Real-time stream processing engine for anomaly detection and data preprocessing, transforming raw digital twin data into actionable insights."
    DTDFVocab:enables MonitoringService, PredictiveAnalyticsService
]

instance BehavioralSimulationEngine : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Simulation environment integrating SysML state machines and MontiArc automata for behavioral modeling and dynamic system state analysis."
    DTDFVocab:enables MonitoringService, OptimizationService
]

instance SimulationCoSimulationFramework : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Co-simulation framework enabling tight coupling between SysML models, Simulink simulations, and physical twins for closed-loop validation."
    DTDFVocab:enables ControlService, SimulationService
]

instance InsightGenerationEngine : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Active processing component interpreting incoming data traces to derive actionable intelligence through formalized ADLs and simulation outputs."
    DTDFVocab:enables DecisionSupportService, VisualizationService
]

instance DataQualityAssuranceService : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Quality assurance mechanism enforcing component interfaces, connectors, and behavioral constraints through formalized ADLs (AADL, SysML)."
    DTDFVocab:enables MonitoringService, PredictiveAnalyticsService
]

// C6: Services
instance MonitoringService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Real-time data aggregation and state tracking service maintaining dynamic system states using behavioral models for condition monitoring and anomaly detection."
    DTDFVocab:provides SystemStateInsight, AnomalyDetectionAlert
]

instance OptimizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Performance tuning service using simulation-based optimization to adjust system parameters while respecting logical and physical constraints from ADLs."

]

instance PredictiveAnalyticsService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Prognostic analytics service leveraging historical and real-time data to forecast failure modes, degradation trends, and operational bottlenecks."
    DTDFVocab:provides FailureModePrediction, RemainingUsefulLifeEstimate
]

instance ControlService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Closed-loop automation service translating digital twin insights into actuation commands for physical system control with adaptive control policies."
    DTDFVocab:provides ControlCommand, AdaptiveControlPolicy
]

instance VisualizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Multi-perspective dashboard service rendering 3D/4D models and interactive diagrams for stakeholder exploration of system states and anomalies."
    DTDFVocab:provides SystemVisualization, AnomalyHighlighting
]

instance SimulationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Digital shadow integration service enabling what-if analyses through simulation of alternative scenarios with heterogeneous tool integration."

]

instance DecisionSupportService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Contextual insight service providing role-based dashboards or API-driven interfaces for accessing processed insights with confidence measures."

]

]

]

]

// C1: Environment
instance DigitalTwinEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains DigitalTwinCoreComponent, DataProcessingLayer, VisualizationInterface
]

    base:desc "Central processing component implementing active processing components for data interpretation, behavioral modeling, and closed-loop feedback."

]

]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system's temporal aspects are governed by the synchronization requirements between physical and digital components, which are implicitly derived from Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) and OOSEM. While explicit synchronization frequencies or latency requirements are not detailed in the provided documents, the integration of heterogeneous modeling tools (e.g., SysML, MontiArc, MathWorks Simulink, or general-purpose languages like C++/Java) implies that the Digital Twin must accommodate varying time scales for different services—ranging from real-time simulation (e.g., in Simulink) to aggregated data processing (e.g., for digital shadows). The temporal granularity is influenced by the abstraction levels (e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, or product/technical layers), where lower layers (e.g., physics-based simulations) may require finer granularity (millisecond-scale) compared to higher layers (e.g., enterprise-level data aggregation, potentially hourly or daily). The documents emphasize that active components within the Digital Twin (e.g., interpreters for incoming data from digital shadows) must handle latency-sensitive operations, though no specific quantitative thresholds (e.g., <100ms latency) are provided."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system's orchestration and coordination are framed within the broader context of Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) and architecture description languages (ADLs). The constellation is structured around the RFLP methodology, which organizes the Digital Twin into distinct layers: Requirements, Functions, Logics, and Physics. Each layer employs specific tools (e.g., SysML for functional/logical architecture, Simulink for physics-based simulation) and follows architecture patterns defined by ADLs like MontiArc or AADL. Resource management is implied through the modular composition of components and connectors, where active components (e.g., interpreters for digital shadows) process data streams in real-time or near-real-time. System-wide coordination is achieved via the integration of these layers, enabling cross-layer communication (e.g., from physics simulations feeding into logical decision-making). The documents do not specify explicit orchestration frameworks (e.g., Kubernetes, microservices) but emphasize the need for heterogeneous stakeholder integration and tool interoperability, suggesting that coordination may rely on middleware or API-based communication between modeling tools."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) system is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, encompassing multiple abstraction layers: **requirements**, **functional architecture**, **logical architecture**, and **product/technical layers**. The **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)** methodology [18, 19] and **SMArDT** [22] are explicitly referenced as frameworks to manage complexity across these stages. Transition management between phases involves **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** [17], **ACME**, **AADL**, and **MontiArc** [23–26], which define components, interfaces, connectors, and composition hierarchies. Behavioral modeling is further refined using **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**, **MontiArc Automaton**, or **state machines in SysML v1.7**, while detailed simulations are integrated via **MathWorks Simulink** [28] or other specialized tools [29, 30]. Lifecycle governance is implicitly supported by **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** [31], ensuring alignment across engineering disciplines, tools, and perspectives. The lifecycle also distinguishes between **passive digital shadows** (one-way data flow) and **active Digital Twins** (bidirectional interaction), with the latter embedding **active processing components** [35–37] to interpret aggregated data traces for contextual abstraction.

"
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system addresses **fidelity and validity** through:
- **Model fidelity requirements**: Achieved via **multi-layered MBSE abstractions** (requirements → functional → logical → physical), ensuring hierarchical consistency across **SysML, ADLs (e.g., AADL), and simulation tools** (e.g., Simulink). Fidelity is implicitly tied to the **granularity of architectural descriptions** (components, connectors, interfaces) and behavioral models (state machines, automata).
- **Validation and verification methods**: Not explicitly detailed, but inferred from the use of **ADLs and simulation tools** for behavioral validation. **Uncertainty quantification** is not mentioned; however, the **Digital Shadow model** implies passive data flows may introduce latency or noise, requiring **active software components** (e.g., interpreters) to mitigate errors (as per [33]–[34]).
- **Error detection and correction mechanisms**: Implicitly handled by **active software components** within the Digital Twin (e.g., interpreters processing aggregated data), though specific algorithms (e.g., Kalman filters, anomaly detection) are not referenced.
- **Quality assurance processes**: Aligned with **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM) to ensure traceability and consistency across models. **No explicit QA frameworks** (e.g., ISO standards) are cited, but the integration of **heterogeneous tools** suggests reliance on **toolchain validation** (e.g., model-to-model transformations).
"
]

    base:desc "The technical network infrastructure for Digital Twin systems is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents. Bandwidth, latency, and reliability mechanisms are not specified, but the requirement for **active components** in Digital Twin systems (as opposed to passive Digital Shadows) implies the need for **low-latency, high-reliability networks** to support real-time or near-real-time control flows. Security protocols are not addressed in the provided context."
]

    base:desc "The deployment infrastructure for Digital Twin systems is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents. However, the context mentions the use of **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies and tools (e.g., SysML, Simulink, general-purpose languages like C++ or Java) during development, implying **on-premise or hybrid deployment models** to accommodate complex system modeling. instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
The document references **industry standards** and **specifications** primarily through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies** and **architecture description languages (ADLs)**. Key standards include:
- **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** [17] for systems engineering modeling,
- **ACME, AADL (Avionics Application Programming Standard Language)** [23, 24] for component-based architecture,
- **MontiArc** [25, 26] for model-driven engineering and automaton-based behavior,
- **MathWorks Simulink** [28] for simulation and modeling (de facto standard in control systems).
Interoperability is addressed through **heterogeneous tool integration** (e.g., combining SysML, ADLs, and simulation tools) and **standardized interfaces** defined by ADLs (e.g., component ports, connectors). Certification requirements are not explicitly mentioned, but compliance frameworks are implied by the **formalized modeling languages** (e.g., AADL’s DO-178C compliance for avionics). No explicit mention of **industry-specific certifications (e.g., ISO 23247 for DTs)** or **interoperability protocols (e.g., OPC UA, DDS)** is provided.
"
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system for the MABI MAX 100 serial robot incorporates several **safety-critical and cybersecurity measures** to ensure operational integrity and compliance with industrial automation standards. The **safety cell** restricts the maximum working range of the first axis to **[-90°, 90°]** and enforces a **maximum speed of |q̇_max| = 75°/s**, mitigating collision risks and mechanical overloads. The system employs **dual position encoders** (motor encoder and secondary encoder post-gearbox) to reduce **backlash and positional uncertainty**, enabling real-time monitoring of drive train elasticity and friction, which is critical for fail-safe operations.

The robot is controlled via **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl. CNC controller**, which enforces **separate axis control** with configurable inputs (position, speed, acceleration) and outputs (target/actual position, derivatives, and motor current). Motor current is converted to **applied torque** using **known motor constants**, allowing for precise torque-based safety thresholds. While the document does not explicitly detail **cybersecurity protocols** (e.g., encryption, authentication, or access control mechanisms), the **safety cell and encoder-based monitoring** serve as **hardware-level safety mechanisms**. For broader **risk assessment**, the system likely relies on **industrial-grade fail-safe protocols** (e.g., ISO 13849 or IEC 61508) to validate operational limits dynamically. However, **data governance policies** (e.g., ownership, anonymization, or compliance with GDPR/industrial data protection regulations) are **not addressed** in the provided context."
]
